content.text: ) are two complementary type system tools for working with the structured dictionaries that dominate Python AI code. LLM messages, tool call results, API responses, and agent state are nearly always plain dicts; TypedDict adds field-level type checking to them. Optional values — results that may be absent, error codes that may be unset — appear everywhere in agent pipelines. Getting these right reduces entire categories of

A TypedDict is a dict subclass declaration that specifies the expected keys and their value types. At runtime it is still a plain Python dict — no additional methods, no validation, no overhead. The value comes entirely from the static type checker, which can now verify that you access only declared keys with the correct types.
TypedDicts can be total (all keys required, the default) or non-total (
, all keys optional). Individual keys can be marked required or optional via the
). You can also inherit from another TypedDict to extend it.
in earlier versions. A value typed as
can be either a string or
. The type checker requires you to narrow the type (
. This is Python's main mechanism for handling absent values safely.

Agent systems deal extensively with structured dictionaries: the message list sent to an LLM API, tool call arguments, tool results, and agent state are all dicts. Without TypedDict, these are typed as
— the checker knows nothing about which keys exist or what their values contain. A typo in a key name, a missing required field, or a
value passed to a function expecting a string will only surface at runtime.
For Optional, the challenge is the billion-dollar mistake:
appearing where a real value is expected. In an agent loop that runs many turns, a single tool that returns
instead of a dict can cause a confusing crash several steps later when the None value is first used. TypedDict and explicit Optional typing catch these issues at development time.
the type checker flags access to undeclared keys and wrong value types in TypedDicts.
a TypedDict class body is a readable schema that doubles as documentation.
TypedDict is purely static; the runtime sees only a regular dict.
TypedDicts work seamlessly with JSON parsing, the Anthropic/OpenAI SDK message formats, and dict comprehensions.
forces callers to handle the absent case before using the value, preventing silent None propagation.
TypedDict does not check that a dict actually has the declared keys at runtime — you need Pydantic or manual checks for that.
TypedDicts are pure data schemas; you cannot add methods to them (use dataclasses or Pydantic for behavioural models).
mixing total and non-total TypedDicts in inheritance chains can confuse both developers and type checkers.
after parsing JSON you still get an untyped dict; you must cast or validate before the type checker treats it as a TypedDict.
The class-based syntax is preferred because it supports inheritance and is readable. The functional syntax (
) is available for keys that are Python keywords.

from __future__ import annotations
from typing import TypedDict, NotRequired

# Total TypedDict: all keys required by default
class ChatMessage(TypedDict):
    role: str       # required
    content: str    # required

# Non-total TypedDict: all keys optional
class ToolCallExtras(TypedDict, total=False):
    tool_call_id: str
    tool_name: str
    arguments: dict

# Mixed: use Required / NotRequired (Python 3.11+ / typing_extensions)
from typing import Required

class AgentMessage(TypedDict, total=False):
    role: Required[str]            # always required
    content: Required[str]         # always required
    tool_call_id: NotRequired[str] # may be absent

# Inheritance: extend a base TypedDict
class AssistantMessage(ChatMessage):
    # Inherits role and content (both required)
    tool_calls: NotRequired[list[dict]]  # additional optional field

# Instantiation: a regular dict literal
msg: ChatMessage = {"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}
print(type(msg))          # <class 'dict'>
print(msg["role"])        # 'user'
The type checker requires you to prove that a value is not None before using it as its concrete type. This is called type narrowing, and

from __future__ import annotations

# Function returning str | None
def find_model(name: str) -> str | None:
    registry: dict[str, str] = {
        "gpt4": "gpt-4o",
        "claude": "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
    }
    return registry.get(name)  # returns None if not found

# Without narrowing: mypy error
# result = find_model("gpt4")
# model_id: str = result   # Error: str | None is not assignable to str

# Pattern 1: early return / raise
def get_model_strict(name: str) -> str:
    model = find_model(name)
    if model is None:
        raise ValueError(f"Unknown model: {name}")
    return model  # type checker knows model is str here

# Pattern 2: walrus operator (3.8+)
def use_model(name: str) -> str:
    if (model := find_model(name)) is not None:
        return f"Using {model}"
    return "Fallback model"

# Pattern 3: default value
def get_or_default(name: str, default: str) -> str:
    return find_model(name) or default

print(get_model_strict("gpt4"))   # 'gpt-4o'
print(use_model("unknown"))       # 'Fallback model'
print(get_or_default("claude", "gpt-4o-mini"))  # claude-3-5-sonnet
The Anthropic and OpenAI message formats are naturally expressed as TypedDicts. Using typed messages catches field access errors statically.

attrs.alt: TypedDict anatomy showing ChatMessage with required str fields (role, content) and optional NotRequired field (tool_call_id: str | None); key modifier legend (Required, NotRequired, total=False, T | None); three Optional patterns: registry lookup returning dict | None, parse returning Literal | None, AgentState with last_error: str | None

content.text: TypedDict provides the schema for structured dicts; Optional (
) expresses fields that may be absent or values that may fail to exist. The two work together: a TypedDict may have some
. The type checker enforces the schema statically while the runtime sees only a plain dict.
any dict passed to the messages parameter of an LLM API should be typed with a TypedDict.
agent run state passed between turns is a natural fit — it has a fixed schema but is serialisable as JSON.
registry lookups, API responses that may be missing, tool results that may fail — all should return
when you need methods, validation, or serialisation support on top of the data.
where you need the value to be a plain dict (for JSON serialisation) rather than a class instance.

from __future__ import annotations
from typing import TypedDict, Literal, NotRequired
import anthropic

class UserMessage(TypedDict):
    role: Literal["user"]
    content: str

class AssistantMessage(TypedDict):
    role: Literal["assistant"]
    content: str

Message = UserMessage | AssistantMessage

def run_simple_agent(system: str, user_input: str) -> str | None:
    client = anthropic.Anthropic()
    messages: list[Message] = [
        {"role": "user", "content": user_input},
    ]

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
        max_tokens=1024,
        system=system,
        messages=messages,  # type: ignore[arg-type]  # SDK uses its own types
    )

    # response.content[0] is a TextBlock; .text is the string
    if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
        return response.content[0].text if response.content else None
    return None

result = run_simple_agent("You are a geography tutor.", "What is the capital of France?")
if result is not None:
    print(result)
Typed tool registry with Optional returns
A multi-turn agent loop maintains fully typed state with TypedDict. Optional fields track errors and intermediate tool results; narrowing keeps the logic clean.

from __future__ import annotations
from typing import TypedDict, NotRequired, Literal
import anthropic
import json

class Message(TypedDict):
    role: Literal["user", "assistant"]
    content: str

class AgentState(TypedDict):
    run_id: str
    messages: list[Message]
    tool_results: dict[str, str]  # tool_use_id -> result text
    last_error: NotRequired[str | None]
    turn_count: int

def make_state(run_id: str, user_input: str) -> AgentState:
    return {
        "run_id": run_id,
        "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": user_input}],
        "tool_results": {},
        "turn_count": 0,
    }

def agent_step(state: AgentState, client: anthropic.Anthropic) -> tuple[AgentState, bool]:
    """Execute one agent turn. Returns (new_state, done)."""
    try:
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022",
            max_tokens=1024,
            messages=state["messages"],  # type: ignore[arg-type]
        )
    except Exception as exc:
        return {**state, "last_error": str(exc)}, True

    new_messages = list(state["messages"])
    if response.stop_reason == "end_turn":
        text = response.content[0].text if response.content else ""
        new_messages.append({"role": "assistant", "content": text})
        return {**state, "messages": new_messages, "turn_count": state["turn_count"] + 1}, True

    # Tool call handling would go here
    return {**state, "messages": new_messages, "turn_count": state["turn_count"] + 1}, False

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
state = make_state("run-001", "Name the capital of France.")
done = False
while not done and state["turn_count"] < 5:
    state, done = agent_step(state, client)

if last_err := state.get("last_error"):
    print(f"Error: {last_err}")
else:
    final_msg = state["messages"][-1]["content"]
    print(f"Answer: {final_msg}")
Using dict[str, Any] instead of TypedDict:
loses all key-level type checking; define a TypedDict whenever the schema is known.
Forgetting to narrow Optional before use:
is a mypy error; always narrow first.
is a type-checker hint only; it does not validate that data actually matches the TypedDict at runtime.
Mixing total and non-total in confusing ways:
on individual fields in a total TypedDict rather than switching
Using Optional[str] for a key that is always present but might be empty:
a key that is always present but may be an empty string should be typed
Name TypedDicts after the data they represent, not where they are used —
on the whole class; explicit per-field optionality is clearer.
For external JSON data, use Pydantic for runtime validation and then work with the validated model; use TypedDict only for in-process data with known schema.
When extending a TypedDict via inheritance, put required fields in the parent and optional fields in the child, not the reverse.
TypedDict has no runtime cost whatsoever — it defines a subclass of
with no extra fields or methods, so creating TypedDict instances is identical to creating plain dicts. Optional (
) similarly has no runtime representation; Python stores the annotation as a string and never evaluates it in normal execution.

import timeit
from typing import TypedDict

class Message(TypedDict):
    role: str
    content: str

# TypedDict creation vs plain dict: identical performance
td_time = timeit.timeit(
    lambda: Message(role="user", content="hello"),
    number=500_000
)
dict_time = timeit.timeit(
    lambda: {"role": "user", "content": "hello"},
    number=500_000
)
print(f"TypedDict: {td_time:.3f}s")
print(f"Plain dict: {dict_time:.3f}s")
# Both print approximately the same value
mode enforces TypedDict key access patterns.
similarly supports TypedDicts; often gives better error messages when you access a missing or misspelled key.
when you need runtime validation; Pydantic can also consume TypedDict definitions via
adds runtime type checking for TypedDicts via decorators — useful for validating external data at pipeline boundaries.

TypedDicts help prevent a class of bugs where agent code silently passes the wrong structure to an LLM API. A dict missing a required field, or with a field of the wrong type, may be accepted by the API (which tolerates extra fields) but cause unexpected model behaviour. TypedDict annotations surface these issues statically.

For data arriving from external sources (LLM outputs, web APIs, user input), TypedDict static checking is insufficient. Combine TypedDict with Pydantic validation at the boundary: parse and validate with Pydantic, then work with the validated data in typed code. Never
user-supplied data to a TypedDict without validation.
Think of a TypedDict as a schema document attached to a dict — it tells the type checker what shape the dict should have, without changing the dict itself. Think of

as a 'box that may or may not contain a T' — you must open the box and check before you can use what's inside. Both tools make implicit contracts explicit, which is the core purpose of the type system.
TypedDict annotates plain dicts with field-level types; at runtime it is still a regular dict.
for fields that may be absent; use
for TypedDicts where all keys are optional.
for values that may be absent; narrow with
TypedDict is ideal for LLM message lists and JSON-compatible data; use Pydantic when you need runtime validation.
is a type-checker hint only — add explicit validation for untrusted data.
TypedDicts have zero runtime overhead; they are purely a static type system feature.
